reasoning: The Lean statement captures the informal claim exactly. The hypotheses `ha`, `hb` — each asserting that `∑_{i<n} |a i|` (resp. `|b i|`) has a limit as `n → ∞` — encode absolute convergence of the two series. The conclusion states that `∑_{i<n} |∑_{j≤i} a_j · b_{i-j}|` has a limit, where the inner sum is precisely the Cauchy product coefficient `c_i = a_0·b_i + a_1·b_{i-1} + ... + a_i·b_0`, and the outer sum of absolute values having a limit expresses absolute convergence of the Cauchy product. This matches Mathlib's `summable_norm_sum_mul_range_of_summable_norm`, which is the standard formulation of Rudin's exercise 3.13. Types (`ℕ → ℝ`), quantifier structure (universal over sequences, existential over the limit), and hypothesis/conclusion content all match the informal statement.
